data.title: AIPI: If You Are Not AI Bear, Then This 38% Yielder Is For You
data.description:
Recently, I circulated an article on the REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) titled FEPI: This 25% Yielder Is About To Generate Even Bigger Income.

In the article, I laid out my justification for why FEPI could be viewed as a significant exception in the income-investing world. It is one of the rare securities, which offers a yield that is meaningfully above 20% and comes with no or very minor NAV erosion.

Usually, whenever a security produces anything that is significantly above 10%, the risks are most likely too high for prudent and sustainable income investors to step in.

However, after the publication of my FEPI piece, I received quite many comments and suggestions to consider the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI), which comes from the same REX Shares family but offers even greater yield than my high-conviction FEPI choice.

The yield on a TTM basis for AIPI stands at ~38%, while for FEPI it is slightly above 25%. So, we are talking about a ~13% monthly yield difference, which is definitely a material number. The delta of 13% itself could move the needle for the majority of income investors (and I'm not even considering the ~25% yielding base).

Now, the yield component is one thing, but for a full picture view, we have to always keep the price (and/or NAV) component with us in calculus. By doing so and comparing FEPI to AIPI, we will see that AIPI has still delivered superior results even though the massive yield would suggest that the opposite scenario should be more likely:

So far the initial story for AIPI looks quite promising.

Let me flesh out the underlying case in more detail.

Thesis

At its core, AIPI is indeed structurally similar to FEPI.

It is an efficiently structured (just 0.65% of the management fee) out-of-the-money covered call ETF, which takes direct equity stakes in certain securities, forming a rather concentrated basket of high volatility names against which options get sold.

And here already we have an important nuance to appreciate that AIPI does not write options against an aggregate or broad index like the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) or the S&P 500 (SPY), which is what many covered call ETF household names such as the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) do.

The security-specific option overlay program is basically a double-edged sword.

On the one hand, it introduces a risk of specific security and their allocation weight-driven underperformance. For example, currently, the largest holding of AIPI is Palantir (PLTR), accounting for more than 10%, while for the QQQ, it is lower. If PLTR nose-dives, AIPI will likely underperform the index.

On the other hand, implementing this kind of approach enables the Fund to capture juicier option premiums due to a higher volatility that is associated with a more concentrated basket of stocks than holding a diversified pool of say, 100 or 500 securities (QQQ and SPY, respectively).

Speaking of AIPI's holdings, the top securities are the following:

And this is where the difference kicks in from FEPI. Namely, AIPI carries a more concentrated exposure towards the AI names, which have over the past couple of quarters and years delivered truly impressive results. They have been volatile, but mostly related to the upside swings.

Here is a relevant excerpt from the fund's prospectus, which describes how AIPI cherry-picks specific AI-biased securities:

Based on their degree of thematic purity, securities are admitted into the Eligible Universe and assigned to one of two categories:

(1) “Purity Leaders,” which are companies with a high relative revenue exposure to AI products, services, and activities;

and (2) “Key Enablers,” which are companies that play a crucial role in facilitating the business application of AI technology, even though their primary business activities may not be solely focused on AI products or services.

Securities in the Eligible Universe are ranked and selected as follow within each category:

A. Purity Leaders: Securities are ranked based on their trading volumes and the top 5 are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are weighted by average daily value traded over the prior 12-months, subject to a 10% cap.

B. Key Enablers: Securities are ranked based on their market capitalization and the top 20 securities are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are equally weighted.

In other words, AIPI is a pure-play bet on AI, just with a covered call overlay angle, which means that the upside is capped by the written option call strike values and the downside is largely open (i.e., the same as for the portfolio holdings), but with a slight nuance that the collected option premiums serve as a partial offset for the potential downside move.

Now, what is important to understand before I transition to the summary section and share my honest opinion on AIPI is how the ETF implements its covered call strategy in practice.

There are two fundamental aspects to this:

  1. As stated above, AIPI writes OTM call options on an individual security level. The magnitude of out-of-moneyness varies among the portfolio securities, but typically we are talking about 5% to 15%. For example, in NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) case, there is roughly 5% of upside participation open. For CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and Micron Technology (MU), it is 8% and 9%, respectively (but MU is already in the money because of the massive run-up that has happened in the past weeks/days). From this we can read that AIPI is not only about income but also about price appreciation, which in the long run comes in handy, serving as an offset for periods when market shocks take place.
  2. The duration of written call options is around 30 days, which gives a nice window of opportunity for stocks to recover in case there has been a steeper correction so that AIPI can avoid locking in losses and setting unfavorable upside caps that might limit its participation in the eventual recovery ride.

We can see how this substance has manifested itself in AIPI's total returns relative to the booming QQQ (AI world):

The presence of OTM covered calls in combination with a more pronounced AI tilt in AIPI's holding base has enabled yield investors to capture very similar returns to the pure-play index (just in an income-friendly fashion).

Granted, if we isolate the period to the past 6 months, we will see that AIPI has delivered ~7.5% in total returns, while the index is up by almost 11%. This divergence is primarily related to the security-selection issue, where AIPI's stronger emphasis on AI names has this time introduced a headwind. Namely, the non-top 10 QQQ index names have been lately delivering better returns than the AI giants.

So, the question is whether this trend will continue. The simple answer is we do not know, but given the rich valuations and the increased chatter in the market around the great rotation - i.e., capital flowing back from large caps into more reasonably valued small caps - we have to be ready that AIPI continues to lag behind the market.

The key is to avoid steep L-shaped sell-offs in the AI space so that AIPI can skip locking in losses after the 30-day OTM option window expires. In my view, considering the past couple of years, where each dip has been followed by a recovery and the underlying case for the AI solutions, I would feel comfortable allocating AIPI in my portfolio as a tactical yield diversifier and enhancer.

The Bottom Line

Honestly speaking, I was quite skeptical about AIPI before I opened the financial documents and started to assess the case deeper.

I see one important and potentially drastic risk in AIPI, which is the massive sell-off of AI names. If that happens and there is a sudden risk-off mode assumed against the richly priced AI stocks, AIPI will get hammered, just as its income. For example, when the tariff tantrum occurred, AIPI dropped from ~$50 per share to $38 per share, and its monthly income distribution amount decreased from $1.45 per share to $1.10 per share.

The good thing here was that AIPI follows an OTM and 30-day expiration strategy, which allowed the vehicle to participate in the recovery and gradually increase the distributions from that "tariff-related plunge" moment. Yet, the rhetorical question is what would happen if the sell-off was not followed by a V-shape recovery, but instead there was an L-shape process in place. Nothing good.

Having said that, we have to recognize that this kind of argument can be applied to FEPI, JEPQ, QQQI, and basically almost any other tech-related covered call index fund (and also for the pure-play indices themselves). The difference in AIPI's case is that under such a pessimistic L-shaped bear market, the impact will probably be much stronger due to the more focused AI strategy.

So, the way I would treat AIPI is like a minor, tactical income enhancer that complements more diversified FEPI and, especially, QQQI exposures. And these AI-related covered call exposures themselves should be kept limited and balanced with covered call vehicles, which harvest vol income from more durable underlying holdings like midstream partnerships (MLPI) and gold (IAUI).

Last but not least, I want to make it clear that AIPI distributes income, which comes in the form of return-of-capital (due to the option premiums being viewed as principal capital) that enables a more tax-efficient treatment.

data.title: LCI Industries: Massive Upside Is Possible
data.description:
The last few months have been exceptionally positive for shareholders of LCI Industries (LCII). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate back in September of last year, the stock has shot up 31.1%. That is drastically higher than the 5.5% increase that the S&P 500 experienced. However, to be fair, since I originally rated the firm a ‘buy’ in October of the previous year, the stock is up a more modest 22.1%. That is above the 19.4% increase that the market enjoyed. But it's not drastically higher.

This overall move can be chalked up to growing revenue, profits, and cash flows. This is interesting considering the challenges that the industry has faced. RV shipments have struggled for years now. But with management now forecasting a more robust recovery than before, and the expectation of additional organic growth over the next couple of years, I am certainly optimistic. I would argue, in fact, that this is a very solid prospect, even if industry conditions deteriorate and negatively affect the company. This is why I have no problem rating it a ‘buy’ at this time.

The picture just keeps getting better

Unfortunately, at this moment, investors don't have access to data covering the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. But we do have data covering through the third quarter. During that time, revenue for the company came in strong at $1.04 billion. This happened to be 13.2% above the $915.5 million the company reported the previous year. Interestingly, this came in spite of the fact that there were certain challenges that the company demonstrated. While for the trailing 12 months ending in September of 2025, the number of wholesale RV units sold came in 4.8% above what the industry had seen the previous year, while the number of retail RV units sold grew by only 0.5%. For the most recent quarter on its own, the industry exhibited weakness. For instance, wholesale unit shipments were down 4.1% year over year, and the retail increase was a paltry 1.3%.

The last few years now have been really challenging for the RV market. The COVID-19 pandemic caused a surge in demand for social distancing. When you add on top of this hefty government stimulus, the end result was a flurry of buying activity in the RV market. In 2020, for instance, RV shipments amounted to only 430,412 units. But the next year, in 2021, they were 600,240 units. This essentially front-loaded significant demand. And by 2023, unit shipments had bottomed out at 313,174 units.

We have started to see a recovery in this market. In 2024, shipments amounted to 333,733 units. Management also increased their guidance for 2025. Right now, they estimate between 340,000 and 350,000 unit sales for this year. That compares to the 337,000 previously estimated at the midpoint. The company now believes that 2026 will see unit shipments of between 345,000 and 360,000 units. The midpoint there translates to 352,500 units. But in their previous forecasts, unit shipments amounted to 349,000.

Short term, I actually think we could see some weakness. After all, as I have detailed in prior articles, I firmly believe the US is heading toward a recession. Consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows, and we can expect that to weigh on buying activity in a market that is purely discretionary and that requires large capital investments. The good news is that, after economic conditions bottom out, we should see some nice recovery here. And that's because interest rates are likely to drop faster than what others anticipate. That should grease the wheels on purchases at some point. So while I think that their forecast for 2026 may be aggressive, I don't think the future for the company or the industry looks bad.

As revenue for the company improved, profitability skyrocketed. Net income jumped from $35.6 million all the way to $62.5 million. It is important to keep in mind, however, that a lot of this improvement came from a $19.7 million gain on the sale of real estate. Actual adjusted net profits grew a more modest, but still very impressive, 35.1% from $35.6 million to $48.1 million. Operating cash flow jumped from $78.4 million to $97.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get growth from $74.2 million to $83.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $85.2 million to $105.9 million.

Management has a long history of doing well in this space. Some of this can be attributed to new products and market share gains. For instance, management said that in 2025, it would capture annualized year over year organic content growth of between 3% and 5%. This is basically the amount of content that goes into each RV. And for 2026, it expects the same. And all of this should be driven solely by innovation opportunities.

This kind of innovation, and the corresponding ability of the company to capture market share from other competitors, has had a huge positive impact on operations. In fact, from the year 2000 to the year 2024, revenue jumped from $288 million to $3.7 billion.

This is a monumental amount of upside, driven by innovation, investments in organic growth opportunities, and the like. The company has made various investments in innovative opportunities, such as anti-lock brakes, certain types of windows, chill lube, and the like. In fact, for the innovations that it discussed in its investor presentation for October of last year, the total addressable markets opened up to it solely by these types of investments amounted to $470 million.

In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of the previous year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all increased. This is positive, especially considering how sluggish the industry has been as of late. What's really exciting, however, is that management expects additional growth opportunities. The firm is targeting $5 billion in revenue by 2027. That compares to the $3.7 billion the business reported for 2024. With that, we are likely to see additional bottom line growth as well. For last year, I anticipate adjusted net profits of $175.5 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $319.2 million, while EBITDA would amount to $390 million. But if the firm achieves its targets, then we would be looking at readings of $327.4 million, $658.2 million, and $787.9 million, respectively.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. For the 2025 figures, the stock is attractive. But just barely. But if we project things out for 2027 based on the guidance management has provided, shares start to look outright cheap. In the chart below, I also compared the company to five other similar firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, it's not all that special relative to other comparable firms. But when it comes to the price-to-earnings approach, it is the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, only one of the companies ended up being cheaper than it is.

In the event that management can achieve their target for 2027, it is likely that upside could be quite large from where it is today. If the firm continues to trade at the same multiples it's currently trading at, we would be looking at an annualized upside of 36.5%. That's based on the current price-to-earnings multiple compared to what I calculated for 2027. On a price-to-adjusted-operating-cash-flow basis, upside would be 43.7% annually. And when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, we would be looking at a 50.6% upside per annum. This is significant. Even if the company can achieve half of this annualized upside, we would be looking at something really impressive.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I remain bullish on LCI Industries. Would I be surprised if short-term economic conditions make financial performance for the business worse? No, I wouldn't be. But when we look at the picture as a whole, I see an attractively priced firm that should continue to achieve nice appreciation. This makes it a clear and obvious ‘buy’ as far as I am concerned.

data.title: Celestica: I Was Wrong - Buy Ahead Of Q4 Earnings
data.description:
I Underestimated Celestica's Operating Leverage Initially

Celestica Inc. (CLS) stock has outrun my October prediction of a highly likely pullback, gaining over 10% since then (the S&P 500 gave us only 3-4% for the same period). I had a "Hold" rating on CLS because I thought the price action had already reflected all the upcoming margin expansion and the business growth from the firm's hyperscale-focused CCS segment. I still don't see excessive margins from CCS ahead, but clearly I was wrong in how I assessed Celestica's operating leverage amid the ongoing shift toward the HPS portfolio. The stock price looks very expensive at first glance, but I think CLS might grow into its current multiples as the structural shift from EMS toward ODM dominance continues and the catalysts for the next few years unfold. That's why I'm upgrading CLS from "Hold" to "Buy".

When Low Margins Don't Matter

Celestica reported for its Q3 2025 numbers a week after my initiation, and it showed $3.19 billion in sales (+27.6% YoY) and $1.58 in adjusted EPS (+51.9% YoY), beating the consensus estimates by 5.1% and 6.03%, respectively, according to Seeking Alpha. Here we can draw a parallel to the growth we've seen in 2024, when CLS achieved a 21% YoY growth in sales amid 58% YoY growth in EPS as the firm's operating margin expanded from 5.5% to 6.5% at the time. As of Q3 2025, the adjusted EBIT margin reached 7.6%, so the fiscal 2025 basically repeated the success of the fiscal 2024 in that regard (and the price action followed a similar path). The company's business model setup doesn't seem to require a tremendous margin expansion to reach a massive operating leverage.

The question now is, "Will Celestica's margin keep climbing in 2026?"

The management said on the call that yes, they're guiding for 7.8% by the end of the fiscal 2026, with an excess of EPS over revenue growth at ~8%:

The outlook for the financial performance of the business in 2026 continues to be very strong. We anticipate revenue of $16.0 billion, representing 31% growth compared to our 2025 outlook. At the segment level, CCS revenue is expected to grow by approximately 40%, driven by strong market tailwinds and program ramps in both our enterprise and communications end markets.

We expect non-GAAP operating margin to expand by 40 basis points to 7.8%, driven by a favorable mix and productivity improvements. Our non-GAAP adjusted EPS is projected to be $8.20, which would represent a 39% increase year-over-year.

Source: Celestica's earnings call for Q3 2025.

So, even a small 0.2% rise in non-GAAP EBIT margin in 2027 should keep the firm's solid operating leverage in place and lead to a superior EPS growth rate in 2026. Personally, I see no signs of slowing and limited risks to margins as long as the medium-term bullish catalysts start to play out and potentially contribute to above-consensus growth rates.

Clear Bullish Catalysts Ahead

We're still in the middle of the Gen AI competitive race, where all LLM providers try to increase the number of parameters for better models' outputs and hence better usability. Thus, the networking market is moving from 800G to 1.6 Terabit (1.6T), requiring a new type of switching. I believe it's the primary bullish catalyst for Celestica's growth ahead as the company has already introduced the DS6000 and DS6001 switch families (they're on the Broadcom Tomahawk 6 chipset), doubling the capacity of existing 800G solutions.

The DS6000 series reduces the number of required switches by 80%, according to another press release, thus "drastically cutting latency and power consumption", which is exactly what hyperscalers will be willing to pay for over the next few years, in my opinion. Also, their DS6001 switch was designed for hybrid air/liquid cooling environments, specifically for AI data centers that run training models consuming megawatts of power, where traditional air cooling is no longer sufficient. I think that as CLS integrates these offerings into its rack-scale solutions, they'll achieve the system level that few competitors can replicate.

Celestica is going to ramp the production of 1.6T programs in 2026 and 2027, thus seeing a very favorable setup for its business beyond just 2026.

Our view for 2027 assumes multiple ramps with hyperscaler customers with new programs supporting the 1.6T upgrade cycle, including scale-up solutions and a next-generation custom ASIC compute platform. We also anticipate the commencement of mass production of our rack-scale custom AI system program with the digital native customer. As a result, we expect these strong growth dynamics to persist. And in support of this, we are aligning our capacity with our customers, assuming that this trajectory continues into 2027.

Source: Celestica's earnings call for Q3 2025

Another bullish catalyst supporting the continuation of the current upcycle is the rise of the custom ASIC compute market, where Celestica has great expertise, as it has been co-developing ASIC-based servers optimized for specific AI workloads.

According to ResearchAndMarkets' study, the custom ASIC market is projected to climb from $27 billion in 2024 to over $43 billion by 2030 (CAGR = 8.23%), so for Celestica, this niche is going to become a great growth addition, in my understanding (likely adding to margin expansion as well).

Another catalyst that adds to Celestica's growth story beyond just 2026 is the planned manufacturing footprint expansion in Texas, where the management expects an increase in power consumption by 10x by 2027. Plus, they're growing in Monterrey, Mexico, and it provides shorter lead times and can start to contribute to the overall growth rates already this year, in my view.

When we combine all the above-discussed catalysts, I think it's becoming clear that Celestica doesn't need to increase its EBIT margin by another 100 bps this year (and next year) to see its EPS grow by 30-40%. The bottom line expansion might come organically and naturally from the top line expansion, and that's where Celestica stands out with its consistency of growth:

When Valuation Peaks Don't Matter

Right now, Celestica is trading above a 50x FY2025 price-to-earnings ratio, which is the peak for the stock when we look back to 10-20 years ago:

But the calendar 2025 is gone, and when Celestica reports for Q4 (in less than 2 weeks), we should start to see FY2026 as its default P/E ratio. When it comes to FY2026, CLS is valued at only 37x. While it still looks expensive to some value investors, I guess, it's not that excessive for the level of EPS growth we're getting. The effective PEG will be below 1x. For FY2027 earnings, which are becoming "1-year forward" now, the stock's PEG drops to ~0.65x, and that's how CLS starts to look underestimated, of the current consensus is spot on.

I dare to conclude here that Celestica's seeming valuation peaks don't really matter. CLS isn't as cheap as it used to be just a few months ago. The rally has given investors the "easy money" already. But if the catalysts that I discussed earlier materialize—at least to some extent—CLS is likely to enjoy another round of positive earnings revisions that should make the forward multiples a steal if the stock price doesn't adjust higher.

I'd put a 10-15% premium on top of today's FY2027 EPS consensus and assume a P/E multiple of 25-30x. The resulting price target for the next 12-16 months arrives at $365.37, and it's 16.5% above the current stock price.

Previewing Celestica's Q4 Earnings

Celestica's management guided for $3.45 billion in revenues at the midpoint, implying a YoY expansion of 36%. The EPS should increase by 56% (to $1.73 at the midpoint), so again, the operating leverage should look good. The market prices premiums on both the top and bottom lines because Celestica usually beats its midpoints.

The history shows that in addition to the midpoints, CLS usually beats the tops of their guidance ranges. And the above consensus is set below the tops ($3.49 billion vs. $3.575 billion and $1.76 vs. $1.81). The odds for beating look very high to me, especially when we assume that the 1.6T transition is already going. My core expectations from Celestica's Q4:

  1. The company beats on both revenue and EPS.
  2. The management raises the previous FY2026 guidance (or at least holds it).
  3. The commentary for 2026 and 2027 sounds bullish.

If all these events happen, CLS's price should naturally be positive.

Risks To My Views

One of the biggest risks to my thesis is that CLS gets ~59% of its total revenues from just 3 key customers, who might decide to switch to another peer over time. The current rally in CLS is driven by the firm's fundamentals, which presumably stem from these three customers' CAPEX plans. So, if they decide to slow down their spending for any reason, Celestica's backlog will decline, and the market will no longer expect the 40%+ growth in its FY2027 EPS. Thus, CLS won't have any justifications for trading as high as it's trading now. Also, we can add geopolitical risks and supply chain disruptions to the list, as Celestica's main operations are conducted outside of the U.S. (they're mainly producing in Asia).

Conclusion

I admit that I was wrong in my previous forecast, where I cited a slowdown in Celestica's margin expansion as the reason for expecting a correction. Now, I see that the level of operating leverage is insanely strong, and CLS doesn't need to grow its EBIT margin by 100 bps yearly to deliver exceptional EPS expansion. What it needs to do is deliver on its plans and ensure the existing catalysts materialize at least partially. Based on the level of execution we've seen in the past, I think that Celestica can do it. I see an undervaluation of 16.5% to the current price, upgrading CLS to "Buy".

data.title: Micron: Like 2023 Nvidia (But Better)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Micron Technology (MU) has appreciated by over 200% in the last 12 months.

Demand for Micron’s products has gone through the roof, as the new age of AI inference necessitates a lot more memory.

Micron’s DRAM and NAND businesses are positioned at the heart of this secular growth trend.

While investors may be tempted to sell now, I think this could be the beginning of a much larger rally, which will mirror Nvidia's (NVDA) ascent in 2023-24.

Risks include cyclicality in memory, new heavy capex commitments, and the AI cycle’s unpredictability.

Micron’s Meteoric Ascent

Over the past 12 months, Micron shares have surged more than 250%.

The thesis is straightforward and compelling.

AI models consume enormous amounts of high-speed DRAM and NAND, and as more companies deploy AI infrastructure, Micron stands to capture a substantial portion of that spend.

This is especially true in the age of AI inference, since AI advancements are made by building upon previous knowledge, which must be stored.

This is something I wrote about in a recent Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) piece.

As AI models grow more complex, the memory footprint scales faster than compute requirements.

In other words, memory needs outweigh compute needs, and therefore, architectures that actually prioritize a memory-first design will outperform going forward.

Source: Did AMD Just Leapfrog Nvidia?

The thing to understand is that there’s a true bottleneck when it comes to memory, so it’s not just that Micro is selling more; it’s selling at higher prices.

This is reflected in the 100% growth in their Cloud Memory segment.

Well, if there’s a memory shortage, surely new supply will appear to balance out the market. Eventually, yes, but there are some very real constraints.

Unlike other semiconductor segments, DRAM and NAND fabrication require both scale and precision, factors that limit new entrants and provide Micron with pricing power during periods of tight supply.

On top of that, Micron has been strategically investing in next-generation technologies, including advanced node DRAM and low-latency NAND, which are increasingly required for AI workloads.

Yes, there are competitors, but ultimately, the memory market is oligopolistic, capital-intensive, and highly specialised.

This isn’t a bottleneck that’s going to be solved with higher prices.

Micron: Like Nvidia, But Better?

After a 200% rally, it would be understandable for investors to consider taking some profit. But this could be a huge mistake, just like it would have been with Nvidia.

As the AI revolution kicked off and GPUs were in short supply, Nvidia’s share price skyrocketed over 400% from the lows in a matter of months.

The stock then began to move sideways, and understandably, a lot of investors thought the bulk of the money had been made.

Clearly, the next two years would prove them wrong, with Nvidia’s stock rallying a further 400%.

Indeed, I see strong similarities between Micron today and Nvidia in 2023.

AI-Driven Secular Tailwinds

GPUs enable AI compute, but AI cannot run at scale without memory.

Every new AI model multiplies memory demand faster than compute demand in some cases.

Training and inference require storing all parameters, activations, and intermediate results, so doubling a model’s size often multiplies memory needs far more than compute. High-bandwidth memory (HBM) limits per GPU mean clusters must add more memory modules or GPUs, further amplifying demand.

This gives Micron a structural tailwind similar to Nvidia’s, but arguably more predictable because AI systems cannot operate without memory.

Oligopoly And Barriers To Entry

Nvidia dominated the GPU market, just as Micron dominates memory.

Well, not quite.

NVIDIA once held over 90% GPU market share, while Micron is closer to 25% today, but the market is dominated by just a few companies.

But the memory market is more capital-intensive, which is a point in Micron's favour.

In this sense, while Nvidia faced intense competition from the likes of Advanced Micro Devices, Micron faces fewer threats because memory supply is extremely hard to scale quickly.

Valuation

By comparison, Micron is still very undervalued compared to Nvidia.

The point we highlighted in the price chart above lines up with this peak in NVIDIA's PE, which at one point was above 100.

The valuation got ahead of itself, which is maybe why the stock was flat for a while. Eventually, however, as earnings began to increase exponentially, the P/E fell and the stock appreciated.

Same setup here in MU if you ask me.

Earnings are expected to quadruple and then keep growing at over 20% the following year, and the market hasn’t priced this in yet.

Micron trades at 30x PE, but this will be much lower next year if the company executes on analyst estimates. This is reflected in the PEG and forward PEG, which are both under 1. Micron also looks attractive based on Price/Cash Flow.

Risks

While the AI memory narrative is powerful, the bullish case for Micron faces significant hurdles.

The main risk is memory cyclicality.

DRAM and NAND markets are notoriously volatile and cyclical. In fact, we can already see that, based on estimates, earnings could fall after 2027.

Micron is also undergoing a massive capital expenditure phase, building new fabs and upgrading nodes.

This is to be expected and not limited to Micron, but it does affect the bull thesis as the investments will hit margins and profitability.

And of course, if overall AI adoption slows, then Micron's current lofty goals will likely not be achieved.

Final Thoughts

Micron’s rally is very much justified, and it could just be the beginning of a much larger rally. All the right ingredients are there, and we’re even seeing insiders buy up more shares.

The way I see it, Micron could easily double in the next year as earnings explode higher, but investors should be aware of the risks laid out above, too.

data.title: FTQI: Tax-Efficient Monthly Income But Not A Good Long-Term Choice
data.description:
Overview

At the close of 2025, I published an article titled 'The Blueprint To Navigate Option ETFs Through 2026'. I laid out many of the traits and characteristics that make an option ETF a great long-term holding, while also detailing the strategies that investors should avoid. I believe that First Trust Nasdaq BuyWrite Income ETF (FTQI) falls somewhere in the middle because the fund has many strong qualities but they are offset by some unfavorable traits. When I previously covered FTQI, I issued a hold rating due to the fund's underperformance against notable peers in the high yield ETF sector. Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to revisit FTQI to specifically focus on what to expect in terms of performance, as well as cover the risks associated with its dividends.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FTQI's share price is slightly above breakeven. The fund has been able to recover from an approximate 20% decline from early 2025, despite its capped upside growth potential. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 13% over the same time frame. FTQI now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 11.3% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. While this makes FTQI a great option for investors that want to prioritize income generation within their portfolio, there are some tradeoffs that must be considered.

FTQI has an excellent history at keeping distributions within a consistent range over the last few years. While this consistency enables long-term investors to compound their annual dividend income, I remain a bit cautious about the untested nature of the fund. I believe that there's a risk for distributions to decline over time if we face a market environment where the indices decline over a prolonged period. A downward market would threaten the success of the fund's underlying strategy and may cause investor capital to erode. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that FTQI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

FTQI's primary objective is to provide a high current income by providing direct equity exposure to U.S. equities, while implementing an option writing strategy against the Nasdaq 100-Index (NDX). According to the latest fund overview, FTQI has total net assets of $772.3M that are spread across 166 different positions at this time. The fund has an expense ratio of 0.76%, which may be high but worth it considering the dividend yield being paid out to investors. FTQI is essentially made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, so the allocation by sector looks quite similar.

For instance, we can see that 51.42% of assets are allocated to information technology companies. This is followed by varying allocations to the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors. However, the large allocation to information technology will certainly be the fund's driver of daily price movements. This can work in the fund's favor during continued bullish momentum, like we are currently experiencing. However, this concentration can also be a major vulnerability for the fund if sentiment around the tech sector shifts and we see a large pullback.

The fund uses a combination of both ATM (at-the-money) and OTM (out-of-the-money) options in order to amplify the level of income generation. I am personally not a big fan of funds that utilize an ATM approach because the selected strike price is typically very close to the current market price of the index. Therefore, an ATM approach severely limits growth potential of the fund. Although an OTM approach also limits growth potential, this strategy typically allows for a greater level of upside growth since the selected strike price is usually between 5% to 15% above the current market price of the index.

However, the most important thing here is to note that the inclusion of an option strategy allows FTQI to harness the volatility of the index and collect option premiums, which are used to fuel the high distributions for shareholders. The written options currently have an average time until expiration of 27 days out, which means there is a suitable amount of time for the fund to capture price growth. However, FTQI is currently writing options against 69.25% of their portfolio value, which leaves a smaller portion of its positions uncapped. In other words, the fund is prioritizing income generation at the expense of growth and since the percentage of assets used for writing call options are a larger portion of its assets, this doesn't allow FTQI to ride the positive momentum of most of its positions uninterrupted.

On the bright side, I like the fact that FTQI provides direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest position at 8.01% of assets. This is followed by many other notable names, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few.

Performance Insights

Since FTQI includes an ATM approach to its option writing, I believe that investors should realistically expect that 90% of their total return will come from distributions, while only the remaining 10% will actually come from price growth over time. To demonstrate this, we can refer to the fund's performance over the last decade. As we can see below, FTQI's share price has only increased by 10.77%, despite the Nasdaq-100 running to all-time highs this year. When including all distributions paid out to investors, the total return jumps up to 108% over the same time frame.

Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that FTQI is best suited for older investors that may want to prioritize income over total return. If you are a younger investor, I want to warn you that the focus on income generation can lead to severe underperformance over a longer holding period. If you want to capture the growth of the market, it makes more sense to hold a more traditional index-tracking ETF, such as the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ).

Additionally, the fund's option writing severely caps upside growth potential over time. However, this strategy offers no protection against market declines. So if the Nasdaq-100 were to decline by 10%, FTQI is likely to experience a very similar decline in price action. However, the main issue is that FTQI will struggle to recover from pullbacks in the market. The ATM option strategy severely limits the fund's ability to climb back from declines, so FTQI may deteriorate capital during a market decline.

As we can see below, QQQ suffered a loss of ~18% at the bottom of the market in early 2025. Similarly, FTQI suffered from a decline that was nearly identical. Once uncertainty cleared, QQQ ended 2025 up more than 20.1% as the tech sector rallied. However, FTQI was only able to break even during the same time frame and I suspect that if the market's rally wasn't so strong, FTQI would still be negative territory today.

Dividend Utility & Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.25 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.3%. This double-digit yield is extremely attractive for investors seeking a high level of income generation on a monthly basis. What I like about FTQI is that even though the payouts can vary based on market conditions, the fund has done a great job at keeping payouts within a consistent range over the last few years. Distributions are directly influenced by the momentum of the market, the success of the option writing, and the implied volatility levels. Therefore, I wouldn't anticipate any sort of traditional dividend raises from a fund like FTQI.

Despite this, I believe that the fund's consistent payout range is something that should still be commended. Since distributions remain in a tight range, this enables investors to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting payouts or adding new capital over time. While this may not fit everyone's needs, this can be an efficient approach for an investor nearing retirement with the objective of creating a supplemental stream of income to support lifestyle expenses.

To help visualize the benefit of this consistent range, I ran a back test of an original $100,000 investment at the start of 2022. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was added to your position after the initial $100k. The back test also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into accumulating more shares of FTQI. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting distributions, we can see that shareholders were able to nearly double their positions annual dividend income over a short period of time.

2022 Dividend Income: $8,543

2025 Dividend Income: $16,152

Additionally, FTQI remains an excellent long-term choice for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 99.26% of distributions on a YTD basis were classified as return of capital. Since FTQI continues to demonstrate its ability to increase its underlying NAV and share price, I would not consider this to be a harmful form of return of capital. In fact, this form of return of capital is beneficial for investors because it allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale, rather than taxes being due upfront on all distributions.

However, there are some risks associated with the distributions. FTQI's high rate of distributions are technically untested through different market cycles. Since distributions are impacted by the success of written options, the payouts can decline if market momentum shifts. If we see a scenario where market indices experience a prolonged downturn, I anticipate that payouts will also decline since the fund's strategy won't be able to produce a high level of option premiums.

Unfortunately, the operating window since FTQI's distributions were elevated is short so we don't have many examples to pull from. However, we can see a slight hint of this vulnerability by looking at how distributions were decreased during the market volatility around President Trump's initial tariff proposals. As the market indices experienced sharp declines, FTQI decreased its payout from $0.21 per share in March of 2025, down to $0.19 per share in April of 2025. While this was a marginal decline, it still resulted in an approximate ~10% decline in payouts. While market momentum eventually recovered and payouts were restored back to their prior level, I believe this is a quick glimpse into how the fund will react during a much stronger and prolonged downturn.

So while payouts have been consistent so far, this can always change in the future. Depending on how severe of a downturn is experienced, there's always the risk that the high yield offered by FTQI is not an efficient hedge against market downturns. In fact, the higher yields can actually exacerbate how rapid FTQI's share price declines if the fund is paying out more than it actually generated in income. So I would advise caution here for investors that want the highest level of reliability from their dividend portfolio during all market cycles.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that FTQI remains a hold at this time. The fund's ATM option writing approach severely limits the appeal of a long term position for me because it caps the upside growth potential of the market. I worry about a scenario where the Nasdaq-100 suffers from a prolonged decline because historical data indicates that FTQI will not be able to recover from this. Although the fund has a history of consistent distributions over the last year, there's the risk of payouts declining if markets experience a pullback. On the bright side, the fund has managed issue distributions in a tax-efficient manner, which is great for investors that want to limit their overall tax burden.

data.title: ADAMO: A 9.25% Senior Note IPO From Adamas Trust
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

We continue our line of work dedicated to turning attention to the newly listed fixed-income securities on the exchange. This time, our informational article will be about Adamas Trust's (ADAM) latest addition to the exchange - the 9.250% Senior Notes due 2031 (ADAMO). We've just issued an article describing our position in (ADAMG). Anyone interested in the company's basic details and the credit quality assessment we recently made can check it out here. As nothing has fundamentally changed in the past couple of days, I'll not be repeating these considerations here and will go straight for the new IPO details. Adamas Trust, under its previous branding, New York Mortgage Trust, had two senior note issues in 2025. This gives us excellent peers for a relative evaluation of the new senior notes issue, the first after the mREIT's rebranding.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information regarding the new baby bond. Here, we will highlight only the metrics that are most important to us and use them in the following analysis.

ADAMO pays 9.25% annual interest in quarterly distributions of $0.5781 per share. Interest on the new baby bond will accrue from and including January 13, 2026, starting with the distribution from April 1, 2026. This means the first interest payment will be irregular in size and will amount to approximately $0.501. Adamas Trust's gross proceeds from the new security are $90 million for a total of 3.6 million notes issued with a $25 liquidation preference each. The new senior debt issue was not scored by any of the three big credit rating agencies on the date of its IPO. It is a mid-term obligation of Adamas Trust with a maturity date of 04/01/2031 and is callable on and after 04/01/2028.

As the issue is just listed, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, ADAMO is priced at $25.05 with a Yield to Maturity calculated by the XIRR function of 9.54%. The tables below show the internal rate of return calculation and how the YTM of the security would change with a price variation around the par value:

In a chart visualization:

Using the same internal rate of return method, the Yield to Call is calculated to be 9.52%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

Adamas Trust's exchange-traded issues

Besides the new issue, Adamas Trust has seven more fixed-income securities listed on the exchange - four preferred stocks and three debt issues. The senior unsecured notes - ADAMI, ADAMG, and ADAMH, being the same type of issues as ADAMO, are the perfect candidates for comparison evaluation.

First of all, we'll compare the IPO credit spreads of these baby bonds to determine how the underwriters' evaluation of Adamas Trust has changed with time.

As noted at the beginning of the article, the senior debt's IPO credit spread is an important indicator for the company as a whole. ADAMH (under the ticker NYMTH at the time) was issued in mid-2025 with a 140 basis points higher risk premium than ADAMG (NYMTG) six months before that. This was a huge jump in a short time, indicating increased caution by the underwriters. Half a year and one rebranding after the issuance of ADAMH, ADAMO has a credit spread of 5.53 points, or more than 50 basis points lower than ADAMH's. Although it hasn't reached the values of ADAMI and ADAMG, it is a step in the right direction, as far as the underwriters are concerned.

ADAMI can be subject to early redemption half a year from now, and ADAMG after a year. Looking at the increasing 5-year forward Treasury yield and the increased rate of financing of ADAM, compared to when these notes were issued, it makes little sense for the Trust to refinance them. This is, according to the current date we have on hand. This, however, is not the case with ADAMH. If the forward yield curve remains the same or downshifts, and the company remains in its current financial strength or improves, it makes sense for ADAMH to be refinanced in October 2027. So, in the comparison that follows, I'll evaluate ADAMI and ADAMG by their YTM metric, and ADAMH by its YTC. As for the new ADAMO, with what the forward 5-year treasury curve indicates for two years for now, for me, it makes more sense that the note is not redeemed early. I'd personally evaluate it here on its YTM metric, but I will throw into the comparison the YTC evaluation too, for those who think otherwise:

Additional consideration here is that with a $90 million book value, the new IPO does not qualify to be included in the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index, and so in the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF). We can expect some selling pressure from the underwriters, and with a large buyer such as PFF out of the picture, a potentially better entry price for ADAMO can be achieved. This is the main reason we are currently invested in the Octopus portfolio in ADAMG. On a change in the current picture, we'll adjust positions accordingly.

Sector Comparison

The comparison below is of the listed mREIT-issued baby bonds by their YTM metric:

All the securities with their metrics:

The chart shows ADAM baby bonds are priced in line with most of the peers in the sector, at least from the companies that are not under some sort of distress, with ADAMO offering its potential investors the longest duration exposure.

Use of Proceeds

We estimate that the net proceeds of this offering will be approximately $86.6 million (or approximately $99.6 million if the underwriters exercise their over-allotment option in full), after deduction of underwriting discounts and commissions and estimated offering expenses payable by us.

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering for general corporate purposes, which may include, among other things, acquiring our targeted assets and/or repayment of existing indebtedness.

Source: ADAMO Prospectus

Special Considerations

If a Change of Control Repurchase Event as defined under “Description of the Notes — Offer to Repurchase Upon a Change of Control Repurchase Event” occurs, we must offer to repurchase the notes at a purchase price equal to 101% of the principal amount plus accrued and unpaid interest to, but excluding, the repurchase date.

Source: ADAMO Prospectus

Conclusion

This is an informational article about Adamas Trust's new senior note IPO, ADAMO. Using this type of article, we target the new exchange-traded IPOs to find new and hopefully better additions to our investment portfolio.

ADAMO's IPO risk premium shows that Adamas Trust is taking steps in the right direction after the quite big IPO credit spread of ADAMH half a year ago. At the moment, we are invested in ADAMG. We expect some sort of underwriters selling pressure in the new security and a potentially better entry price in it.

data.title: AGNC Investment: 3 Floating Preferred Shares Offer Over 9% Yield
data.description:
Introduction

AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) is a real estate investment trust whose primary form of investment is mortgages, also known as an mREIT. Along with the high-yielding common shares, the company offers five different preferred shares. One of these (AGNCL) is offering a fixed rate of under 8%, with the opportunity to float late next year. Another (AGNCM) is floating at a dividend yield of under 9%.

The other three preferred shares (AGNCN), (AGNCO), and (AGNCP) are all floating and at yields of over 9%. I last wrote a bullish piece on the company’s floating preferred shares back in April. Today, I’m bullish on all the preferred shares, but I believe that investors should consider putting new money towards either of the three preferred shares yielding over 9%.

AGNC Financial Results

AGNC invests in agency-backed mortgage securities, which are mortgages that are insured by GSEs (like Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac). While the risks to mortgage failures are mitigated, the company’s risks associated with interest rates were not. After buying mortgages that were initiated at record low fixed rates during the pandemic, the company saw its interest expenses jump and threaten to overcome their interest income when the Federal Reserve raised interest rates to battle inflation.

In 2023 and 2024, the company saw interest income rise, but interest expenses surged to eclipse interest income. The result was seven consecutive quarters of negative net interest income (interest income less interest expense) from the first quarter of 2023 through the third quarter of 2024. Lower interest rates in late 2024 through this year have allowed interest expenses to cool down, and despite a pullback in net interest income last quarter, the level is in line with where the company was in 2019, prior to the pandemic.

Fortunately, during times of interest rate volatility, AGNC deployed hedges designed to mitigate the effects of higher interest rates. These hedges brought down the cost of borrowing and generated earnings for AGNC to keep the company solvent. At one point, the cost of borrowing after the effects of hedging was more than 400 basis points lower than the true borrowing costs.

Recently, AGNC has started to shrink its hedge, likely out of the expectation for interest rates to continue to fall. The decline in the size of the hedge has allowed the average cost of funds to slowly creep up. With asset yields facing the headwinds of renewed monetary easing and borrowing costs creeping up due to lower hedging, the company’s net interest spread has pulled back.

Why Not Invest in Common Shares?

AGNC recently experienced a sizable rally in its common shares, fueled by the hope of GSEs purchasing mortgage bonds, lowering interest rates, and increasing demand. While I would love to share in the optimism, I believe the common shares are too risky. For starters, over the last ten years, AGNC has had to cut its dividend three times, making the income angle on its shares unreliable.

In addition to the dividend history, management has had a difficult time returning value to shareholders. Over the last six years, tangible book value has been on a steady decline downwards, going from nearly $18 per share in 2019 (and again in 2021) down to around $8 per share presently. The exceptionally low interest rates of the pandemic were only good enough to briefly return tangible book value back to pre-pandemic levels. Based on the history of book value per share, I have no interest in investing in a strategy where my dividends are partially funded by the initial investment.

Risks to AGNC Preferred Shares

Interest rate volatility poses the biggest current risk to AGNC. The risk becomes especially concerning when coupled with the company reducing its hedging activities. Should inflation cause interest rates to reverse course, AGNC could be caught in a situation where its hedging minimally protects its earnings and book value takes a bigger dive than it has in the last six years. It is important to note that the company would need to suspend its common share dividends before impairing preferred dividends, so while the risk is real, preferred shareholders would have a shield in the common dividends being eliminated before preferred dividends can be cut.

Conclusion

Recent events surrounding the mortgage market have given AGNC Investment shareholders reason to be optimistic about the future. The company has seen improvements in net interest income and lower interest rates in hopes that it will improve mortgage demand. Instead of dealing with the struggles of tangible book value, income investors can still get 9% yields in any of three preferred shares being offered by AGNC. I consider all three preferreds to be roughly the same, so between AGNCN, AGNCO, and AGNCP, investors should look at the yields at the time of purchase and select the highest one.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
